request.question: What number of male band members do both Juliette and the Licks and Civil Twilight have?

title: Holy Weather
Dusk
Honey G (band)
Monday Kiz
Civil Twilight (album)
Juliette and the Licks
Civil Twilight (band)
Civil twilight (disambiguation)
Belt of Venus
Every Single Day (band)
sentences: Holy Weather is the second album by South African alternative rock band Civil Twilight.

Written and recorded in the years following their debut album, Civil Twilight, the album was released 26 March 2012 in both the United States and other regions including the United Kingdom, Canada and South Africa.
The album has received generally positive reviews, with many comparing the outfit to early-generation U2.
Dusk occurs at the darkest stage of twilight, or at the very end of astronomical twilight after sunset and just before night.

Pre-dusk, during early to intermediate stages of twilight, there may be enough light in the sky under clear-sky conditions to read outdoors without artificial illumination, but at the end of civil twilight, when the earth rotates to a point at which the center of the sun is at 6° below the local horizon, artificial illumination is required to read outside.
The term "dusk" usually refers to astronomical dusk, or the darkest part of twilight before night begins.
Honey G (Korean: 허니지) is a South Korean male band formed in 2012.
Their members consist of Bae Jae-hyun, Park Ji-yong and Kwon Tae-hyun.
Honey G is currently signed to Chungchun Music.
Honey G released their first album, "1st Album" on August 22, 2013.
Monday Kiz (Korean: 먼데이 키즈) is a South Korean male band formed in 2005.
Their members consist of Lee Jin-sung, Han Seung-hee and Im Han-byeol.
Monday Kiz is currently signed to Rainbow Bridge World.
Monday Kiz released their first album, "Bye Bye Bye" on October 6, 2005.
Civil Twilight is the debut studio album of Civil Twilight.
It was officially released 6 April 2010 in South Africa and on 13 April 2010 in the United States and United Kingdom.
It features many alternative rock and classical influences.
Many have drawn parallels between the band's debut and the early work of U2 along with Muse.
Steven McKellar's vocals have been compared to that of Radiohead's Thom Yorke and Jeff Buckley.
During its original independent release by the band in 2007, the album was originally titled "Human".
Juliette & The Licks is an American rock band led by actress and singer Juliette Lewis.
Other band members have included guitarist Craig Fairbaugh and guitarist Emilio Cueto, bassist Jason Womack and drummer Ed Davis.
Their popular songs include "You're Speaking My Language" and "Hot Kiss".
The band broke up in 2009.
In 2015, Lewis announced the band would be reunited on July 23rd for a show in Los Angeles.
The future of the band past this show was unknown until Juliette announced, via her Facebook account on Feb 1st 2016, that the band will play a gig in London's Heaven on May 4th 2016.
In June of 2016, Juliette Lewis announced a solo tour leaving the future of The Licks undetermined.
Civil Twilight is a four-piece rock band from Cape Town, South Africa, consisting of the brothers Andrew and Steven McKeller, plus Richard Wouters, and Kevin Dailey.

They are signed to Wind-up Records, and have released three studio albums — their self-titled debut album, "Civil Twilight" (2010), "Holy Weather" (2012), and "Story of an Immigrant" on July 10th 2015.
Civil twilight is the brightest phase of twilight, when the sun is less than 6° below the horizon.

The Belt of Venus, Venus's Girdle, twilight wedge, or antitwilight arch is an atmospheric phenomenon visible shortly before sunrise or after sunset, during civil twilight, when a pinkish glow extending roughly 10–20° above the horizon surrounds the observer.
Every Single Day (Korean: 에브리싱글데이 ), is a popular all male band from South Korea formed in 1997.
The group debuted with three original members: Moon Sung-nam (singer and bassist), Jung Jae-oo (guitarist), and Gang Moon-chul (drummer).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What number of male band members do both Juliette and the Licks and Civil Twilight have?
hop1_docs: Juliette and the Licks | Juliette & The Licks is an American rock band led by actress and singer Juliette Lewis.  Other band members have included guitarist Craig Fairbaugh and guitarist Emilio Cueto, bassist Jason Womack and drummer Ed Davis.  Their popular songs include "You're Speaking My Language" and "Hot Kiss".  The band broke up in 2009.  In 2015, Lewis announced the band would be reunited on July 23rd for a show in Los Angeles.  The future of the band past this show was unknown until Juliette announced, via her Facebook account on Feb 1st 2016, that the band will play a gig in London's Heaven on May 4th 2016.  In June of 2016, Juliette Lewis announced a solo tour leaving the future of The Licks undetermined.
Four on the Floor (Juliette and the Licks album) | Four on the Floor is the second and final full-length album by Juliette and the Licks.

Do You See What I See? (song) | "Do You See What I See?"  is the fourteenth single by Australian Pub rock band Hunters & Collectors, released in 1987.  It was released ahead of the album on August 1987 in both 7" and 12" formats.  It was released as the first single from Hunters & Collectors fifth album "What's a Few Men? ".  "Do You See What I See?"  peaked at number 33 on the ARIA Charts and at number 13 on the Recorded Music NZ.

Civil Twilight (band) | Civil Twilight is a four-piece rock band from Cape Town, South Africa, consisting of the brothers Andrew and Steven McKeller, plus Richard Wouters, and Kevin Dailey.  They are signed to Wind-up Records, and have released three studio albums — their self-titled debut album, "Civil Twilight" (2010), "Holy Weather" (2012), and "Story of an Immigrant" on July 10th 2015.

Civil Twilight (album) | Civil Twilight is the debut studio album of Civil Twilight.  It was officially released 6 April 2010 in South Africa and on 13 April 2010 in the United States and United Kingdom.  It features many alternative rock and classical influences.  Many have drawn parallels between the band's debut and the early work of U2 along with Muse.  Steven McKellar's vocals have been compared to that of Radiohead's Thom Yorke and Jeff Buckley.  During its original independent release by the band in 2007, the album was originally titled "Human".

Craig Fairbaugh | Craig Fairbaugh (born March 11, 1978) is an American musician, singer and songwriter who is best known for playing with the rock band +44 (pronounced "plus forty-four").  The band was made up of Mark Hoppus and Travis Barker (both of Blink-182) and Shane Gallagher (of The Nervous Return).  Fairbaugh has joined Juliette and the Licks full-time as guitarist and back-up vocalist.  He is also the guitarist and vocalist of the goth rock band Mercy Killers.
Lick (band) | Lick was a band on Chicago's Invisible Records in the 1990s.  Members have played with Pigface, The Final Cut, Project Elf, Sister Machine Gun and more.

response.summary_1: Evidence: Juliette and the Licks — "Other band members have included guitarist Craig Fairbaugh and guitarist Emilio Cueto, bassist Jason Womack and drummer Ed Davis." Civil Twilight — "Civil Twilight is a four-piece rock band from Cape Town, South Africa, consisting of the brothers Andrew and Steven McKellar, plus Richard Wouters, and Kevin Dailey." Bridge/missing fact: passages show each band has four additional/named members, but do not explicitly state the sex of each named member or give an explicit "male members" count. Next retrieval direction: fetch sources (band pages, member bios) that confirm the gender of Craig Fairbaugh, Emilio Cueto, Jason Womack, Ed Davis, Andrew McKellar, Steven McKellar, Richard Wouters, and Kevin Dailey — or locate an explicit statement/count that each band has four male members.

Other band members have included guitarist Craig Fairbaugh and guitarist Emilio Cueto, bassist Jason Womack and drummer Ed Davis.  Their popular songs include "You're Speaking My Language" and "Hot Kiss".  The band broke up in 2009.  In 2015, Lewis announced the band would be reunited on July 23rd for a show in Los Angeles. They are signed to Wind-up Records, and have released three studio albums — their self-titled debut album, "Civil Twilight" (2010), "Holy Weather" (2012), and "Story of an Immigrant" on July 10th 2015.
Emilio Cueto | Emilio Raul Cueto (born September 10, 1977) is a guitarist who has played in the rock bands Rama Duke and Juliette and the Licks.

Craig Fairbaugh | Craig Fairbaugh (born March 11, 1978) is an American musician, singer and songwriter who is best known for playing with the rock band +44 (pronounced "plus forty-four").  The band was made up of Mark Hoppus and Travis Barker (both of Blink-182) and Shane Gallagher (of The Nervous Return).  Fairbaugh has joined Juliette and the Licks full-time as guitarist and back-up vocalist.  He is also the guitarist and vocalist of the goth rock band Mercy Killers.

It was officially released 6 April 2010 in South Africa and on 13 April 2010 in the United States and United Kingdom.  It features many alternative rock and classical influences.  Many have drawn parallels between the band's debut and the early work of U2 along with Muse.  Steven McKellar's vocals have been compared to that of Radiohead's Thom Yorke and Jeff Buckley.  During its original independent release by the band in 2007, the album was originally titled "Human".
Craig McKellar | Craig McKellar (born 8 December 1949) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Richmond and Melbourne in the Victorian Football League (VFL) during the 1970s.
Andrew McKellar | Dr. Andrew McKellar (February 2, 1910–May 6, 1960) was a Canadian astronomer.
response.summary_2: Both Juliette and the Licks and Civil Twilight have four male band members. Civil Twilight is explicitly described as the four-piece of brothers Andrew and Steven McKellar plus Richard Wouters and Kevin Dailey; Juliette and the Licks lists Craig Fairbaugh and Emilio Cueto (both confirmed male in their bios) along with Jason Womack and Ed Davis as the other four members.
